title: A Personalized Spatiotemporal Human Activity Recognition Framework in an Edge-Based Federated Learning Approach
description:
Federated Learning (FL) revolutionizes machine learning, emphasizing privacy preservation through local model training. However, centralized FL has limitations, steering research toward decentralized models.

This study explores a decentralized privacy-preserving deep federated learning with the integration of edge computing, our focus extends to Human Activity Recognition (HAR). Challenges in this integration include privacy-sensitive sensor data, diverse user behaviors, and the need for personalized models. Existing studies propose solutions, but incorporating spatiotemporal data remains a challenge due to heterogeneity and communication overhead. Our research introduces an edge-based federated learning approach for HAR, contributing to spatiotemporal data extraction and integration.

Lightweight, resource-efficient techniques like CNN, RNN, t-SNE, and dynamic scheduling are employed to address challenges, ensuring precise HAR while prioritizing privacy in a dynamic landscape.

title: Salary Data Prediction and Analysis Using Machine Learning
description: Developed a predictive model for tech employee salaries using four years of Stack Overflow data. Implemented and compared ML models (Linear Regression, Decision Tree, Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost) using MAE, MSE, and R² metrics. Integrated the model into production for real-time predictions via Streamlit and visualized salary trends pre- and post-pandemic with Matplotlib, supported by detailed documentation.

title: Cognitive Science and Artificial Intelligence
description: Authored an APA-format literature review on AI creativity, rationality, and theory of mind, synthesizing research, assessing methodologies, and identifying gaps in the field.

title: Optimizing XGBoost for Predicting Student Academic Success: A Unified Approach to Feature Engineering and Model Evaluation
description: Accurate prediction of student academic performance is essential for early intervention, personalized learning, and enhancing educational strategies. This study explores the use of the XGBoost algorithm for predicting student performance based on behavioral and academic data. While prior studies have demonstrated XGBoost's efficiency, they often focus on isolated optimization techniques—such as feature selection or hyperparameter tuning—tested on different datasets, lacking a unified evaluation framework. This paper addresses this gap by replicating and adapting six state-of-the-art XGBoost approaches into a regression-focused analysis on a single student performance dataset. Additionally, an enhanced modeling pipeline is proposed, integrating regression-based class balancing, Optuna-driven hyperparameter optimization, and threshold-based feature selection. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed unified approach outperforms baseline methods, achieving an R² score of 0.9685 and the lowest RMSE of 3.6764.

publication.conference: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence & Networking (QPAIN)

title: Evaluating the Usability of Ride-Sharing Applications for Semi-Literate Users: A Study in Bangladesh
description: Ride-sharing applications have become integral in urban transport, yet their usability for semiliterate users remains underexplored. This study evaluates three popular platforms—Uber, Pathao, and Obhai—using Cognitive Walkthrough (CW) and the System Usability Scale (SUS) in the context of Bangladesh. Pathao achieved the highest usability with an average SUS score of 80.25 (grade B), followed by Uber at 79.5 (grade B), indicating good usability with minor issues in navigation and feedback. Obhai scored 59 (grade D), revealing challenges such as unclear instructions, confusing navigation, and inadequate feedback. Based on these findings, a redesigned prototype, Go Jatra, was developed with simplified language, clear ride confirmations, transparent payment flow, and post-ride summaries. SUS evaluation of Go Jatra showed an improved score of 81.2 (grade A), demonstrating enhanced usability for semiliterate users. The study highlights the importance of intuitive design, clear guidance, and culturally sensitive interface elements in making ride-sharing applications more accessible.

publication.conference: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)

title: Enhancing Disaster Prediction Using Graph-Based Centrality Features and Explainable Machine Learning on FEMA Data
description: Natural disasters often occur in complex, interdependent patterns, where the occurrence of one disaster may trigger or amplify another. Accurate prediction of such co-occurrence patterns can significantly improve preparedness, mitigation strategies, and resource allocation. This study utilizes the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) disaster dataset, comprising 5,098 records with heterogeneous categorical and numerical features. To capture structural relationships, a bipartite network is constructed linking disaster identifiers to declaration types. Multiple centrality measures—degree, closeness, betweenness, eigenvector, and Katz—are computed and used as novel features to represent relational dependencies. These graph-based features are integrated into supervised machine learning models for disaster classification, enhancing predictive performance. To ensure interpretability, explainable AI techniques including TreeInterpreter, Partial Dependence Plots (PDPs), and ELI5 permutation importance are applied to decompose predictions, analyze feature effects, and rank feature importance. Results demonstrate that centrality-based features significantly improve model performance while revealing non-linear relationships between disaster characteristics and declaration outcomes. The study highlights the effectiveness of combining graph theory and explainable AI for data-driven disaster management and decision-making.
contributions: Introduced centrality-based features for disaster prediction
Improved classification performance using graph features
Applied XAI techniques for model interpretability
Revealed non-linear relationships in disaster patterns

title: A Deep Learning and Federated Learning-Based Intrusion Detection System with Data Balancing and Explainability
description: The continued expansion of network infrastructures has increased their vulnerability to cyber intrusions, necessitating robust intrusion detection systems (IDS). However, real-world network traffic is often highly imbalanced, leading to underrepresentation of critical attack classes during model training. This study proposes a deep neural network (DNN)-based IDS using the CICIDS2017 dataset and evaluates three advanced data balancing techniques: SMOTified-GAN, GANified-SMOTE, and SMOTE-Tomek. Among these, GANified-SMOTE achieved the best balance with an F1-Macro score of 0.967. To ensure privacy preservation, the system integrates federated learning and evaluates four optimization algorithms: FedAvg, FedProx, FedAvgM, and FedNova. Experimental results demonstrate that FedProx performs best under non-IID data conditions, achieving an F1-Macro score of 0.9668. Additionally, explainable AI (XAI) techniques, including SHAP, LIME, and Morris Sensitivity Analysis, are employed to enhance interpretability and provide insights into model decision-making. The study highlights the effectiveness of combining data balancing, federated learning, and explainability for building reliable and secure IDS systems.
